$schema: Compact profiles for every Inhabitants dropdown option. Used by MapGenerator + buildImagePrompt to force Sonnet + gpt-image-1 to honour the chosen race when naming places, picking architecture, and shaping culture. Random / null inhabitants bypass the lookup. Non-civilised entries (Undead, Beasts, Monsters, Demons, Fey, Abandoned) describe lair/aesthetic rather than settlement architecture; settlement-context maps with these inhabitants are unusual but valid (a cursed town, a fey grove etc.).

humans.label: Humans
humans.architectural_style: Medieval European — timber-frame and wattle, stone foundations, thatched or slate roofs, market squares with stone wells. Practical and varied; cosmopolitan towns mix styles.
humans.naming_examples: Period-appropriate Anglo-Saxon, Frankish or Celtic — Aldric, Edwin, Roselyn, Cedric, Mira, Wystan, Greycliff, Mossford, Brindleshire
humans.cultural_notes: Adaptable, faction-prone, short-lived but ambitious; mercantile guilds, knightly orders, and parish temples are common
humans.material_culture: Iron and steel arms, woollen cloth, brewed ale, common armours; varied religions

elves.label: Elves
elves.architectural_style: Tall, organic forms integrated with living trees and earth. Treetop platforms, woven branch bridges, flowing curves, silver and jade accents, vine-wrapped pillars, moon-glass lanterns. No nautical, no Anglo-Saxon stonework.
elves.naming_examples: Aelindra, Thaelmar, Vaelorin, Nelyndor, Sylvaris, Ithelys, Caelthorn, Mythriel, Aerendril, Faelorin (musical phonetics, soft consonants; avoid blunt single-syllable names)
elves.cultural_notes: Long memory, reverence for nature and ancestors, oral lore, ranger / druid prominence, slow ritual rhythms, distrust of haste
elves.material_culture: Silverwork, moon-glass, inscribed living wood, bowyer and fletcher crafts, elven wine, woven leaf-thread garments

dwarves.label: Dwarves
dwarves.architectural_style: Stone-cut, angular, fortified. Underground halls, runic engravings, vaulted chambers, brass and iron fittings, geothermal forges, mead-halls with carved beams
dwarves.naming_examples: Thorin, Grimna, Durbarak, Khelvar, Brunhildur, Borin Stonehand, Hilda Ironfoot, Korgan, Drathna (clan-name suffixes — '-axe', '-foot', '-stone' — common)
dwarves.cultural_notes: Honor-bound, clan-oriented, craftsmanship-obsessed, long grudges, ale culture, mining heritage, ancestor worship
dwarves.material_culture: Mithril work, geared mechanisms, runic stonework, ale-houses, weapon forges, gem-cut signet rings

halflings.label: Halflings
halflings.architectural_style: Low, rounded, earthen. Hill-burrows with round doors, pastoral gardens, communal greens, smallholder farms, no fortifications
halflings.naming_examples: Pip Underhill, Rosie Bramblefoot, Tobold Greenbottle, Marigold Tealeaf, Wendel Burrows, Otho Goodbarrel (cosy compound surnames)
halflings.cultural_notes: Hearth-focused, food-obsessed, peaceful, community-minded, suspicious of grand ambition, generous to travellers
halflings.material_culture: Pies and ales, simple textiles, gardening tools, comfortable furniture, pipe-weed, painted shutters

gnomes.label: Gnomes
gnomes.architectural_style: Whimsical and mechanical. Geared workshops, copper-and-brass details, clockwork accents, ornate weather-vanes, glass-blower kilns, tunnel-and-burrow hybrid dwellings
gnomes.naming_examples: Fizban Cogspring, Brinkwhistle, Toggletop, Wendel Tinkertongue, Pip Sparkmender, Glim Wickwarder
gnomes.cultural_notes: Curious, inventive, prone to elaborate practical jokes, mechanically gifted, illusionist tradition, restless tinkerers
gnomes.material_culture: Clockwork mechanisms, optical instruments, brass fittings, alchemical curios, painted whirligigs

half_orcs.label: Half-Orcs
half_orcs.architectural_style: Functional and fortified. Rough stone or heavy timber with reinforced gates, watch-platforms, blood-red or ochre banners, totemic markers at thresholds, war-trophies displayed
half_orcs.naming_examples: Grull, Karkash, Drogga, Mor'gak, Brakthul, Ushtaar, Rena Ironjaw, Vorga Bonecutter (hard consonants, guttural)
half_orcs.cultural_notes: Warrior-coded but often integrated; clan or warband loyalty, ancestor veneration, strength-respected hierarchies, distrust of intrigue
half_orcs.material_culture: Heavy weapons, hide and chain armour, war drums, totem carvings, hearty stews and roast meats

half_elves.label: Half-Elves
half_elves.architectural_style: Hybrid — elven elegance grafted onto human practicality. Cosmopolitan settlements mixing styles; arched windows and woven roofs alongside stone halls
half_elves.naming_examples: Aelric, Mariel, Caelan, Lirien, Rhys Vaelmar, Sela Thornlight (softer human names with elven phonetics)
half_elves.cultural_notes: Cosmopolitan, bridge-builders, often outsiders to both parent cultures, traders, diplomats, scholars
half_elves.material_culture: Both elven and human crafts present, often trade-focused; cross-cultural cuisine

mixed_races.label: Mixed Races
mixed_races.architectural_style: Cosmopolitan blend reflecting a multi-racial population. No single dominant aesthetic — district styles may vary; markets and shared civic buildings draw from several traditions
mixed_races.naming_examples: Mixed naming from multiple cultures — Anglo-Saxon humans alongside elven, dwarven and halfling names side by side
mixed_races.cultural_notes: Multi-cultural, often trade hubs or large cities where many races coexist; complex politics, polyglot streets
mixed_races.material_culture: All represented; specialty crafts cluster in racial guild quarters

humanoids.label: Humanoids (Goblinoids)
humanoids.architectural_style: Crude, scavenged, makeshift. Lean-tos against ruins, hide stretched over bone frames, raider's palisades, captured banners and stolen tapestries used as decoration
humanoids.naming_examples: Skrag, Gruk, Yazak, Brog the Maimed, Vex Tooth-Pull, Mok-Rakh (short, harsh, often single-syllable)
humanoids.cultural_notes: Tribal, raider-oriented, opportunistic; warband hierarchy based on strength, brutal initiation rites
humanoids.material_culture: Salvaged armour, bone fetishes, crude weapons, painted skull totems

undead.label: Undead
undead.architectural_style: Decayed and necrotic. Once-grand structures now rotting; lichen-stained stone, blackened timbers, bone-strewn courtyards, faded heraldry, ever-present mist
undead.naming_examples: Place names allude to loss or death — Mournhold, Pale Hollow, the Stillquarter, Ash-Vale, Lichensward, Greypall
undead.cultural_notes: No living inhabitants; routines persist from memory — ghostly market days, processions to long-gone temples, repeated death-throes
undead.material_culture: Rusted tools, mouldered ledgers, sealed coffins, candles guttered down to wax-pools, untouched feast tables

demons.label: Demons
demons.architectural_style: Infernal corruption layered onto whatever stood before. Brass and obsidian, asymmetric arches, brazier-lit altars, burn-scarred stone, blood-grooved channels in floors
demons.naming_examples: Infernal-tinged or corrupted forms of older names — the Smouldering Quarter, Ashen Gate, Veshkar's Hollow, Kael-Mor
demons.cultural_notes: Hierarchical and predatory; pact-bound cultists, soul-debts, oaths sealed in flame; ordinary inhabitants either fled or corrupted
demons.material_culture: Blackened iron, obsidian glassware, brand-marked livestock, contract scrolls in brittle parchment

fey.label: Fey
fey.architectural_style: Whimsical and impossible. Toadstool dwellings, twisted-bark cottages, mist-bridges, mirror-pools, hedges grown into archways; geometry that doesn't quite resolve
fey.naming_examples: Pun-tinged or riddle-like — the Glassmoth Glade, Thistlewend, Mossbinder's Crossing, the Hush, Three-Lantern Hollow
fey.cultural_notes: Capricious, riddle-bound, bargain-fixated, mercurial moods; tradition matters more than truth; trespassers face puzzling tests
fey.material_culture: Glamoured leaves passing as gold, dewdrop jewellery, ribbon and feather work, music-bottled-in-glass

beasts.label: Beasts
beasts.architectural_style: No constructed architecture — dens, scratching-posts, nesting hollows, well-worn game trails, prey-piles, claw-marked territory boundaries
beasts.naming_examples: Place names describe terrain and the resident beast — Bear's Hollow, Greatclaw Ridge, the Roaring Pass, Wolf-tongue Vale
beasts.cultural_notes: Animal behaviour patterns — pack hierarchies, hunting circuits, seasonal migrations; no language, no economy
beasts.material_culture: Bone-strewn middens, fur-lined dens, scent-marked perimeters

monsters.label: Monsters
monsters.architectural_style: Lair-style. Co-opted ruins, twisted natural caverns, web-strewn passages or fume-clogged tunnels depending on the dominant monster; trophies of prey scattered throughout
monsters.naming_examples: Place names warn of what lairs there — the Sundered Hold, Maw of Vergrak, the Wyrm-Roost, Hollowfang Crag
monsters.cultural_notes: Solitary apex predators or small grouped horrors; territorial, ambush-oriented, no commerce — adventurers come here for slaying, not parley
monsters.material_culture: Bones of prey, half-digested treasure mounds, scratched glyphs claiming territory

abandoned.label: Abandoned
abandoned.architectural_style: Pre-existing architecture frozen at the moment of departure. Doors left ajar, half-finished meals on tables, ivy creeping over windowsills, decay measured in years not centuries
abandoned.naming_examples: Names refer to what the place WAS — Old Greycliff, the Forgotten Vineyards, Ardent's Rest, Last-Light Hamlet
abandoned.cultural_notes: No active inhabitants. Whatever drove the population out (plague, raid, curse) lingers as evidence — graves, broken doors, scrawled warnings — not as active threat
abandoned.material_culture: Whatever was left behind; tools rusting where they fell, larders gone foul, ledgers unfinished mid-line
